Bohr model diagram showing atom with nucleus and quantized electron orbits (n=1,2,3...). Only certain discrete orbits allowed, explaining discrete atomic spectra. Electron jumping between orbits emits or absorbs photon with energy equal to orbit energy difference. Quantization resolves classical physics problems.

Energy level diagram for hydrogen atom with energy plotted vertically. Horizontal lines represent allowed energy levels (n=1,2,3, to infinity). Downward arrow shows electron transition from higher to lower level, emitting photon. Energy difference equals photon energy. Example shows Balmer series transition.

Comprehensive hydrogen energy diagram showing all major spectral series. Ground state (n=1) at bottom, excited states above. Lyman series: arrows from higher levels to n=1. Balmer series: arrows to n=2. Paschen series: arrows to n=3. Each series occupies different spectral region (UV, visible, IR).

Three-part diagram explaining electron wave nature. Part (a): standing waves on string with nodes at ends, wavelength related to length. Part (b): string formed into circle - constructive interference requires integer wavelengths fit circumference. Part (c): wrong wavelength causes destructive interference - orbit impossible. Shows wave basis for orbit quantization.

Two circular orbit diagrams showing electron matter waves. n=3 orbit: exactly 3 wavelengths fit circumference. n=4 orbit: exactly 4 wavelengths fit circumference. Integer wavelength requirement explains Bohr's quantization condition through de Broglie wave-particle duality.

Electron probability cloud for hydrogen ground state. Darker regions indicate higher probability of finding electron. Densest near nucleus (Bohr radius), fading with distance. Electron can be closer or farther than Bohr radius but unlikely at large distances. Replaces definite orbits with probability distribution.

Lithium-7 nucleus showing 3 protons (one color) and 4 neutrons (another color) clustered together. Mass number A=7, atomic number Z=3. Actual mass 7.016 u. Demonstrates nuclide notation and nuclear composition. Lithium-7 is isotope of lithium with specific neutron number.

Nucleons (protons in one color, neutrons in another) tightly packed in nucleus. Nuclear forces hold nucleons together against electrostatic repulsion. Nucleons resist separation and compression. Nucleus volume equals sum of nucleon volumes, shown by different colored spheres.

Alpha decay diagram showing parent nucleus emitting alpha particle (2 protons + 2 neutrons = helium-4 nucleus). Daughter nucleus has atomic number reduced by 2, mass number reduced by 4. Demonstrates transmutation - element changes identity.

Beta decay diagram showing neutron in nucleus converting to proton, electron, and neutrino. Electron (beta particle) ejected from nucleus. Atomic number increases by 1, mass number unchanged. Specific type shown is β⁻ decay. Demonstrates weak nuclear force interaction.

Gamma decay diagram showing excited nucleus dropping to ground state. Energy released as high-energy photon (gamma ray). No change in atomic or mass number - only energy state changes. Nucleus shown before and after with photon emission indicated by wavy arrow.

Three-part comparison of radiation penetration. Part (a): higher energy radiation penetrates farther. Part (b): radiation has smaller range in high electron-density materials. Part (c): alpha particles have smallest range, beta particles greater range, gamma rays greatest range. Shows relative penetrating power.

Two-part figure. Part (a): photograph of handheld Geiger counter device. Part (b): diagram of Geiger tube internals showing voltage between cylinder and central wire, ionization of gas by radiation, ion/electron movement creating current pulse detected and registered as count.

Graph showing exponential radioactive decay. Vertical axis: number of nuclei N. Horizontal axis: time in half-lives. Curve decreases exponentially - each half-life reduces number by half. N₀ at t=0, N₀/2 at one half-life, N₀/4 at two half-lives, etc. Mathematical equation N = N₀e^(-λt) describes decay.

Multi-step fission diagram. Step 1: neutron absorbed by large nucleus adds energy. Step 2: nucleus deforms like liquid drop, narrows in middle. Step 3: fewer nucleons in contact, Coulomb repulsion breaks nucleus into two parts plus several neutrons. Energy released. Step 4: Additional neutrons can trigger more fissions - chain reaction basis.

Chain reaction diagram showing exponential growth. One fission produces neutrons triggering multiple new fissions. Each generation produces more fissions - 1 becomes 2, becomes 4, becomes 8, etc. Geometric progression can be controlled (reactor) or runaway (bomb). Requires each fission produce at least one additional fission.

Pressurized water reactor schematic showing containment, pressure vessel, reactor core with fuel rods and control rods, primary coolant loop, steam generator, secondary loop with turbine and generator, cooling tower. Control rods adjust neutron flux for self-sustaining reaction. Water moderator slows neutrons - loss of water stops reaction (safety feature). Heat generates electricity.

Graph of binding energy per nucleon vs mass number. Iron peak shows maximum binding. Left of iron: fusion releases energy (combining light nuclei increases BE/nucleon). Right of iron: fission releases energy. Fusion curve shows mass converted to energy when light nuclei fuse because products have higher BE/nucleon.

Graph showing potential energy vs separation distance for two nuclei. Coulomb repulsion creates barrier (hump) at intermediate distances. If nuclei have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome barrier, they fuse and drop into deep attractive well at small separations, releasing energy. Explains temperature requirement for fusion.

Cross-section of Sun showing fusion regions. Core at center where fusion occurs (highest temperature, sufficient hydrogen). Energy diffuses slowly outward through layers. Neutrinos escape immediately. Fusion primarily at helium core boundary. Negative feedback maintains stability - if too hot, expansion cools; if too cool, contraction heats.

Gun-type bomb diagram showing cannon barrel design. Conventional explosive forces one subcritical uranium mass into another subcritical mass. Combined mass exceeds critical mass, initiating rapid chain reaction. Energy yield depends on uranium amount and time before assembly disintegrates. Design used for uranium bombs.

Implosion bomb design showing spherical plutonium core surrounded by shaped explosive charges. Simultaneous detonation compresses plutonium to supercritical density. More sophisticated than gun design, required for plutonium due to its properties. More efficient fission weapon design.

Photograph of Trinity nuclear test explosion, July 1945, New Mexico. Massive mushroom cloud rising into sky. First nuclear weapon detonation in history. Led to development of atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, ending World War II.

Hydrogen bomb schematic showing fission bomb (trigger) surrounded by fusion fuel (deuterium/tritium). Fission explosion provides temperature and pressure for fusion ignition. Neutrons and gamma rays heat and compress fusion fuel, create tritium from deuterium. Outer ²³⁸U shell reflects neutrons, boosts yield by fissioning. This thermonuclear weapon design is vastly more powerful than fission alone.

Tokamak fusion reactor diagram showing toroidal (doughnut-shaped) vessel with magnetic field lines (purple curves) confining high-temperature plasma (purple). Magnetic fields prevent hot plasma from touching vessel walls. When fusion occurs, high-velocity neutrons escape magnetic confinement, carrying energy to be collected.

Medical brain scan image showing computer-enhanced view of brain using radiopharmaceutical tracer. Different regions show varying tracer concentrations. Demonstrates nuclear medicine imaging for diagnosis and disease monitoring.

Gamma camera diagram showing components: lead collimator with parallel holes directs gamma rays, scintillator crystal produces light flashes when gamma rays strike, photomultiplier array converts light to electrical signals, computer constructs image from detector output. Used for radiopharmaceutical imaging.

Photograph of patient lying in SPECT scanner with circular array of gamma detectors rotating around patient. Single-photon emission computed tomography combined with CT provides 3D images of radiopharmaceutical distribution in body. Improved resolution over simple gamma camera.

PET scanner diagram showing circular detector ring around patient. Positron from radiotracer annihilates with electron, producing two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions. Detectors record coincident gamma ray pairs, determining emission line location. Computer analyzes multiple events to form precise 3D image.

Radiation therapy diagram showing cobalt-60 gamma ray source rotating around patient on table. Radiation beams from different angles all pass through tumor at common crossing point, concentrating dose there while distributing dose to surrounding tissue across larger volume. Geometric technique for well-defined tumors.

Photograph of Large Hadron Collider tunnel at CERN on Swiss-French border. Shows massive cylindrical detector equipment and infrastructure in underground tunnel. LHC is particle accelerator designed to study fundamental particles through high-energy collisions. World's largest and most powerful particle collider.

Hierarchical diagram showing six levels of matter from macroscopic to subatomic. From largest to smallest: solid object, molecule (connected atoms), atom (nucleus with electron cloud), nucleus (cluster of nucleons), nucleon (proton or neutron), quark (fundamental particles inside nucleon). Shows how matter structures at different scales.

A proton and neutron exchanging pion particle. Pion created through temporary mass-energy violation, travels from proton to neutron, then recaptured. Virtual particle exchange mediates strong nuclear force between nucleons. Pion shown as red orb between particles.

Two parts showing virtual particle exchange. Part (a): two charged particles exchanging virtual photon, mediating electromagnetic force. Part (b): shows virtual photon cannot be directly observed - observing it disrupts exchange and alters force. Virtual particles exist only during exchange. Quantum mechanical force transmission.

Feynman diagram with time vertical axis, distance horizontal axis. Two positively charged particles (vertical lines) exchange virtual photon (wavy line connecting them). Particles repel due to photon exchange. Diagram read bottom to top shows particle trajectories diverging after exchange. Demonstrates quantum mechanical electromagnetic repulsion.

Feynman diagram showing proton and neutron (vertical lines) exchanging positive pion (curved line). Pion mediates strong nuclear force. Time flows upward, pion transfers momentum and force between nucleons.

Photograph of LIGO control room in Hanford, Washington. Computer monitors and equipment for detecting gravitational waves. Scientists search for graviton through LIGO and particle accelerator experiments like LHC. Evidence may be indirect - like footprints in sand rather than seeing graviton directly.

Van de Graaff generator diagram showing: battery (A) supplies charge to pointed conductor, points spray charge onto moving insulating belt, pointed conductor (B) in large sphere picks charge off belt. Charge accumulates in sphere to very high voltage. Shows principle of electrostatic charge accumulation and acceleration.

Two parts. Part (a): synchrotron diagram showing circular ring of magnets and accelerating tubes. Frequency of accelerating voltage increased to keep particles traveling same distance in shorter time. Magnetic field increased to maintain fixed radius. Very large size needed for high energies. Part (b) and (c) show charged particle in acceleration gap between tubes for positive and negative charges.

Fermilab accelerator schematic showing two concentric rings (not to scale). Inner and outer rings accelerate particles in opposite directions. Protons and antiprotons brought to collision at interaction points. Beam collision geometry for particle physics experiments.

Fermilab accelerator schematic showing two concentric rings (not to scale). Inner and outer rings accelerate particles in opposite directions. Protons and antiprotons brought to collision at interaction points. Beam collision geometry for particle physics experiments.

Aerial photograph of Fermilab showing circular accelerator rings with circular ponds nearby for waste heat dissipation. Facility accelerated protons and antiprotons to 1 TeV (trillion electronvolts). Shows scale of particle physics infrastructure.

Schematic showing electron scattering from proton revealing three point-like quarks inside. Similar to Rutherford's alpha scattering discovering nucleus. High-energy electrons have small wavelength to resolve quark-scale details. Scattering pattern confirms quark model of proton structure.

Illustration showing two quarks exchanging gluon. Gluon (spring-like line) carries strong force between quarks. Exchange can change quark colors but not flavors. Example: down quark changes from red to green while up quark changes from green to red. Colors not specific to quark types.

Particle composition diagram. Top: proton (uud quarks) and neutron (udd quarks) - both baryons with three quarks. Bottom: positive pion (u and anti-d) and negative pion (d and anti-u) - both mesons with quark-antiquark pair. Arrows show quark spins. Colors add to white. Shows fundamental difference between baryons and mesons.

Detailed Feynman diagram showing proton-neutron interaction at quark level. Quarks exchange gluons mediating strong nuclear force. Time flows upward. Shows fundamental quantum chromodynamics mechanism underlying nuclear force.

Bubble chamber photograph showing particle tracks. Photon (no track) enters, produces electron-positron pair with curved tracks in opposite directions due to magnetic field. Pair production demonstrates antimatter creation. Tracks curve oppositely proving opposite charges. Evidence of matter-antimatter symmetry.

Standard Model chart organizing all fundamental particles. Color-coded groups: six quarks (up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom), six leptons (electron, muon, tau and their neutrinos), four gauge bosons (photon, W, Z, gluon). Each entry shows symbol and mass. Higgs boson (discovered 2012) shown separately. Chart provides information on particle stability.

Feynman diagram showing weak force interaction. Electron and neutrino (vertical lines) exchange Z boson (wavy line connecting them). Similar to electromagnetic and strong force diagrams but with massive Z boson mediating weak interaction. Time vertical, position horizontal.

Graph showing force strengths vs energy/distance. Horizontal axis: energy in GeV (higher energy = smaller distance). Vertical axis: force strength. At low energies, forces very different. At high energies: weak and electromagnetic unify (electroweak). Higher still: strong force joins (GUT). Ultimate unification includes gravity (TOE). Shows force unification at increasing energies.

Galaxies moving away from Milky Way (MW at center). Arrows radiate outward indicating galaxy recession. More distant galaxies move faster, approaching speed of light. Purple background with dots representing stars. Demonstrates universe expansion from Big Bang, Hubble's law.

Timeline of universe from Big Bang to present. Horizontal axis: time from 10⁻⁴³ seconds to 10⁹ years. Vertical axis: energy from 10¹⁹ GeV to 1 GeV. Color gradients show eras: Planck epoch, GUT era, Quark era, Hadron era, Lepton era, Nucleosynthesis, Atomic era, Galaxy formation. Major events annotated: force unification, particle formation, atom formation, stars/galaxies. Shows cosmic evolution driven by particle physics.

Two statements about particle-antiparticle mass relationship. First (correct): particles and antiparticles have same mass. Second (incorrect): particles and antiparticles have different mass. Also shows Tevatron accelerator layout with proton/antiproton sources, rings, and collision detector.

Explanation of color change in quark interaction. Down quark emits gluon carrying red-antigreen color charge, causing quark to change from red to green. Demonstrates quantum chromodynamics color charge exchange mechanism.

Complete periodic table of elements organized in standard format. Elements grouped by numbered columns (Group 1-8). Each entry contains atomic number, chemical symbol, atomic weight, and element name. Color coding indicates states of matter and properties: Metal (Red), Metallic (Yellow), Liquid (Blue), Nonmetal (Green), Gas (Purple). Standard reference for all chemical elements and their properties.

A line graph titled “Energy vs Time.” The horizontal axis is labeled Time, ranging from 0 to about 15 units. The vertical axis is labeled Energy, with values tightly clustered between approximately 0.4985 and 0.5001. A single blue curve labeled ENERGY oscillates smoothly in a repeating, sinusoidal pattern. The energy reaches periodic peaks near 0.5000 and troughs near 0.4987, showing small, regular fluctuations around an average value of about 0.4995. The amplitude of the oscillation is very small, indicating that the energy is nearly constant over time with slight periodic variations.

Passengers are seated inside an airplane cabin while one person stands in the aisle reading a document. Below the cabin, a side view of the airplane and a horizontal coordinate axis illustrate motion along the x direction. The airplane moves from an initial position of six point zero meters to a final position of two point zero meters. The change in position is calculated as final position minus initial position, giving negative four point zero meters. The negative value indicates the airplane’s displacement is four meters toward the left, which is the negative x direction.

A woman stands in front of a classroom chalkboard in two positions to illustrate motion along a horizontal axis. In the first position she is at an initial location of one point five meters. In the second position she has moved to a final location of three point five meters. A horizontal axis labeled x points to the right, indicating the positive direction. The change in position is calculated as final position minus initial position, giving positive two point zero meters. This shows the woman moved two meters to the right, which is the positive x direction.

Two perpendicular axes intersect at the origin to form a coordinate system. The horizontal axis represents the x direction, with positive x pointing to the right and negative x pointing to the left. The vertical axis represents the y direction, with positive y pointing upward and negative y pointing downward. The arrows on each axis indicate the positive directions for x and y used to describe position and motion in two dimensions.

An airplane is shown above a horizontal coordinate axis that represents motion along the x direction. Several horizontal arrows illustrate individual changes in position during different time intervals. These arrows represent separate displacements labeled change in x one, change in x two, change in x three, and change in x four. Some arrows point to the right, indicating motion in the positive x direction, while others point to the left, indicating motion in the negative x direction. A longer arrow labeled total change in x shows the overall displacement resulting from adding all the individual displacements together. The diagram demonstrates how multiple changes in position combine to produce a total displacement along a single axis.

Three graphs compare position, velocity, and speed as functions of time for the same motion. The first graph shows position in kilometers versus time in hours. The position starts at zero kilometers at zero hours, increases linearly to three kilometers at zero point three hours, and then decreases linearly back to zero kilometers at zero point five hours. This indicates the object moves away from the starting point and then returns. The second graph shows velocity in kilometers per hour versus time. From zero to about zero point three hours the velocity is positive twelve kilometers per hour, indicating motion in the positive direction. At zero point three hours the velocity changes to negative twelve kilometers per hour and remains constant until zero point five hours, indicating motion in the opposite direction. The third graph shows speed in kilometers per hour versus time. The speed remains constant at twelve kilometers per hour for the entire time interval, showing that the object moves at the same speed even though the direction of motion changes.

A house labeled home is shown on the left and a building labeled store is shown on the right. A straight path between them is labeled three kilometers. One arrow points from the home to the store, representing traveling three kilometers to the store. A second arrow points back from the store to the home, representing the return trip along the same path. The total change in position is labeled zero because the starting point and ending point are both at the home, illustrating that displacement is zero even though distance was traveled.

A house labeled home is shown on the left and a building labeled store is shown on the right. A straight path between them is labeled three kilometers. One arrow points from the home to the store, representing traveling three kilometers to the store. A second arrow points back from the store to the home, representing the return trip along the same path. The total change in position is labeled zero because the starting point and ending point are both at the home, illustrating that displacement is zero even though distance was traveled.

Two graphs compare acceleration as a function of time.The first graph shows acceleration in meters per second squared versus time in seconds as a smooth curve that rises and falls slightly between about one and two meters per second squared over a time interval from zero to five seconds. A horizontal line labeled average acceleration indicates the mean value of the changing acceleration over the interval. The second graph shows acceleration in meters per second squared versus time in seconds using a step pattern. The acceleration begins at about three meters per second squared, then changes to negative two meters per second squared around one second. Later it returns to zero, increases to about one and a half meters per second squared, then rises to about five meters per second squared before dropping to a negative value near negative four meters per second squared around six seconds. The graph illustrates how acceleration can change abruptly at different times instead of varying smoothly.

Three stacked graphs show position, velocity, and acceleration plotted against time. The first graph shows position in meters versus time in seconds. The plotted points form a curve that starts near zero meters at zero seconds, increases gradually, then rises more steeply until about thirty seconds, and begins to level off near about two hundred seventy meters around forty five to fifty seconds. The second graph shows velocity in meters per second versus time in seconds. The line increases from zero meters per second at zero seconds to about eight meters per second at around twenty seconds. It stays roughly constant near eight meters per second until about forty seconds, then decreases sharply to zero around forty five seconds. The third graph shows acceleration in meters per second squared versus time in seconds. The acceleration begins around zero point four meters per second squared for the first twenty seconds, drops to approximately zero for the next interval, and later becomes negative before returning toward zero near the end of the time range.

A horizontal line represents motion along the x direction. The left end of the line is labeled initial position x zero, and the right end is labeled x equals question mark, indicating an unknown final position. Above the line, a green arrow pointing to the right is labeled average velocity equals four point zero zero meters per second. On the right side of the image, a coordinate system shows the x axis pointing to the right and the y axis pointing upward.

A graph titled displacement versus velocity for a given time shows displacement in meters on the vertical axis and average velocity in meters per second on the horizontal axis. Several plotted points form a straight line that rises from the lower left toward the upper right. The points are connected by a line, showing a linear relationship between average velocity and displacement.

A horizontal line represents motion along the x direction with the displacement labeled change in x equals question mark. On the left side, the initial velocity is labeled v zero equals thirty point zero meters per second with a green arrow pointing to the right indicating the direction of motion. On the right side, the final velocity is labeled v f equals zero meters per second and is marked by a green point. Below the line, two acceleration values are listed: acceleration for dry conditions equals negative seven point zero zero meters per second squared, and acceleration for wet conditions equals negative five point zero zero meters per second squared.

A horizontal position axis labeled position x in meters shows the stopping distance of a car. In the top illustration, a car braking on dry pavement stops after about sixty four point three meters, while a car braking on wet pavement stops after about ninety meters. In the lower illustration, a shaded region labeled reaction time shows the distance the car travels before braking begins. After this reaction distance, the braking distance is shown. The total stopping distance is about seventy nine point three meters on dry pavement and about one hundred five meters on wet pavement. Multiple car icons mark the positions where the car stops under the different conditions.

Three stacked graphs show vertical motion over time. The first graph shows vertical position in meters versus time in seconds. The plotted points rise from zero meters at zero seconds to a maximum of about eight to nine meters near one point five seconds, then decrease steadily and pass below zero to about negative five meters by around three seconds. The second graph shows velocity in meters per second versus time in seconds. The line decreases steadily from about positive twelve meters per second at zero seconds, crosses zero near about one point two seconds, and continues downward to about negative fifteen meters per second around three seconds. The third graph shows acceleration in meters per second squared versus time in seconds. The acceleration is shown as a constant horizontal line at about negative ten meters per second squared throughout the time interval.

Two side by side diagrams show the vertical motion of an object thrown from the edge of a cliff. In the left diagram, a person stands on a cliff and throws an object straight upward from an initial position labeled y zero equals zero with an initial velocity of thirteen meters per second upward. The object rises to a maximum height labeled y one equals eight point one zero meters, then falls past a point labeled y two equals six point four zero meters while moving downward. The object continues falling below the cliff edge to a position labeled y three equals negative five point one zero meters with a downward velocity of about negative sixteen point four meters per second. Arrows indicate the upward and downward directions along the vertical axis. In the right diagram, the same motion is shown for an object thrown straight downward from the cliff. The initial position is labeled y zero equals zero with an initial velocity of negative thirteen meters per second downward. The object reaches a position labeled y equals negative five point one zero meters with a velocity of about negative sixteen point four meters per second. Arrows indicate motion downward along the vertical direction.

An illustration and several graphs show the motion of a falling sphere. On the left, a hand releases a small sphere that falls downward in a series of positions. Next to the illustration is a table with three columns labeled vertical position y in meters, velocity v in meters per second, and time t in seconds. The table lists values showing position becoming more negative and velocity becoming increasingly negative as time increases from zero to about zero point five seconds. Below the table, three graphs display the motion. The first graph shows position versus time, where the position decreases in a curved line as time increases. The second graph shows velocity versus time, where the velocity decreases linearly from zero to about negative five meters per second. The third graph shows acceleration versus time, where acceleration remains constant at approximately negative ten meters per second squared over the time interval

A multi part physics diagram illustrates projectile motion and the components of velocity. In the top section, a ball follows a curved path through the air. At several points along the path, arrows show the velocity vector and its components. The horizontal component of velocity labeled v x is shown pointing to the right at each position. The vertical component labeled v y points upward while the ball rises, becomes zero at the highest point, and points downward as the ball falls. The combined velocity vector is shown at different angles along the trajectory. A second section shows several equally spaced positions of the ball along a horizontal line, each with a right pointing arrow labeled v x, indicating constant horizontal velocity. Another section shows vertical motion with several stacked positions of the ball. Arrows labeled v y point upward near the beginning and downward as the ball moves downward.The final small diagram shows the velocity vector separated into horizontal component v x and vertical component v y relative to x and y coordinate axes.

Earth is shown with a cannon positioned on top of a tall mountain firing projectiles horizontally with different initial velocities labeled v zero. Several curved paths extend around the planet, where some trajectories curve downward and intersect the surface while others travel farther before falling. One path continues around Earth to form a circular orbit, and arrows along each path indicate the direction of motion around the planet.

Two diagrams compare projectile trajectories for different launch speeds and launch angles. In the top diagram, three projectiles are launched from the same point at an angle of forty five degrees. The initial speeds are labeled thirty meters per second, forty meters per second, and fifty meters per second. Each projectile follows a curved path through the air, with greater speeds producing longer horizontal ranges. The ranges shown are about ninety one point eight meters, one hundred sixty three meters, and two hundred fifty five meters. A small coordinate system indicates the x direction horizontally and the y direction vertically.In the bottom diagram, projectiles are launched with the same initial speed of fifty meters per second but at different angles labeled fifteen degrees, forty five degrees, and seventy five degrees. Each path forms a different curved trajectory. The ranges shown include about one hundred twenty eight meters and two hundred fifty five meters along the horizontal direction.

Motion along a circular path is shown with two nearby points labeled B and C on the circle and a center point labeled A. Lines from the center to B and C represent the radius labeled r, and the angle between them is labeled change in theta. The curved arc between B and C is labeled change in s, and the straight line between them is labeled change in r. At point B, a green arrow labeled v one shows the velocity tangent to the circle. At point C, another green arrow labeled v two shows the velocity at that point along the path.Above the circle, a small vector triangle shows the relationship change in v equals v two minus v one. The vectors labeled v one and v two are arranged tip to tail, and the resulting arrow represents the change in velocity.

A spring is attached between a fixed support and a movable block, with the spring’s length labeled x. When the block is pulled to the right by a hand, the spring stretches by an additional distance labeled change in x. A red arrow labeled restoring force points to the left, indicating the direction of the force exerted by the spring opposing the pull. Below, a spring scale with numbered markings shows the spring stretched by a distance labeled change in x while a horizontal arrow labeled restoring force points toward the left, representing the force measured by the scale.

A child sits in a small wagon while two people push it forward along a flat surface. Arrows show the forces acting on the wagon: two applied forces pointing to the right labeled F one and F two, friction pointing to the left labeled f, the normal force pointing upward labeled N, and the weight pointing downward labeled w. Another panel shows the forces combined into a single net force pointing to the right. To the right, a free body diagram represents the wagon as a single point with arrows showing the same forces: normal force upward, weight downward, friction to the left, and applied forces to the right. In a second scenario below, one adult pushes the wagon with a single force labeled F adult pointing to the right. A corresponding free body diagram again shows the normal force upward, weight downward, friction to the left, and the applied force to the right.

A person pushes two different objects to the right with a force labeled F. In the first case, the person pushes a basketball with mass labeled m one, and an arrow labeled acceleration a one points to the right. In the second case, the person pushes a car with mass labeled m two, with a force arrow pointing to the right and an acceleration arrow labeled a two. Below these scenes, simplified free body diagrams show a single point with an arrow labeled F pointing to the right for each object. Text indicates that the free body diagrams for both objects are the same, representing the applied force acting on each object.

A person pushes a lawn mower across grass while walking behind it. A red arrow labeled net force points horizontally to the right, indicating the direction of the overall force acting on the mower as it moves forward.

A rocket powered vehicle moves to the right along a flat surface with an arrow labeled acceleration pointing to the right. Several arrows labeled thrust point forward from the rear of the vehicle, while an arrow labeled friction points backward. Vertical arrows show the normal force upward and the weight downward acting on the vehicle. Below the vehicle, a free body diagram represents the object as a point with arrows showing the same forces: normal force upward, weight downward, friction to the left, and multiple thrust forces to the right.

A swimmer pushes against a pool wall underwater while preparing to move away from the wall. An arrow labeled direction of acceleration points away from the wall. The swimmer’s feet press against the wall, and an arrow labeled force of wall on feet points away from the wall. On the right side, a free body diagram represents the swimmer as a point. Arrows show the buoyant force upward, weight downward, and the force of the wall on the swimmer pointing away from the wall.

A person pushes a loaded cart forward across a floor. Arrows indicate several forces acting on the system. A force from the person on the cart points forward, friction on the wheels points backward, and forces between the person’s feet and the floor act in opposite directions. The diagram labels two systems of interest: one including the person and cart together, and another focusing on the cart and objects on it. On the right side, two free body diagrams represent these systems as points with arrows showing the forces acting on each system. The arrows indicate the normal force upward, weight downward, friction to the left, and applied forces to the right from either the floor or the person pushing the cart.

A hand presses down on a soft object placed on a table. In the first case, an upward arrow labeled force from the hand acts on the object, while a downward arrow labeled weight acts toward the table. In the second case, the object rests on the table without the hand pressing it. An upward arrow labeled normal force acts from the table on the object, and a downward arrow labeled weight acts toward the table. Below, simplified free body diagrams represent the forces acting on the object in each situation, showing upward forces from the hand or table and a downward weight force.

A hand holds a vertical rope attached to a block with mass labeled m. The rope pulls upward on the block with a force labeled tension, shown by an upward arrow labeled T. A downward arrow labeled weight shows the gravitational force acting on the block. To the right, a free body diagram represents the block as a point with two forces: an upward arrow labeled tension and a downward arrow labeled weight.

The top figure shows the internal structure of a human finger with the bones, extensor muscle, flexor muscle, and tendons labeled. The tendons run along the finger and attach the muscles to the bones. Red arrows labeled tension point along the tendons, indicating the direction of the pulling forces transmitted through the tendons when the muscles contract. The bottom figure shows a bicycle frame and handlebar system. Several arrows labeled tension appear along different parts of the frame and cable system, including near the handlebars and along the frame tubes. These arrows indicate forces acting along the cables and structural members of the bicycle, illustrating how tension forces can be transmitted through connected components.

A person stands on a scale inside an elevator that is suspended by a cable. In the first panel, several forces acting on the elevator system are shown with arrows. A tension force in the cable points upward. The normal force from the scale on the person points upward, while the person’s weight points downward. Additional downward arrows represent the weights of the scale and the elevator. In the second panel, the person is identified as the system of interest. The forces acting on the person are shown: an upward force from the scale and a downward weight force. On the right, a simplified free body diagram represents the person as a point with two vertical forces: an upward force from the scale and a downward weight force. A small coordinate axis indicates the vertical direction labeled y and horizontal direction labeled x.

Two hands hold a flexible object stretched vertically between them. The upper hand moves the object sideways from its central position. The shape of the object curves as it is displaced, and motion lines show it moving back and forth. A label marks the equilibrium position at the center, with arrows pointing left and right to indicate displacement on either side of that position.

Graph showing the relationship between force and displacement for a spring. The vertical axis is labeled Force, F in newtons, and the horizontal axis is labeled Displacement, x in meters. A straight line passes through several plotted points, increasing from the origin upward to the right, showing that force increases linearly with displacement. The slope of the line is labeled k equals slope equals rise over run. Dashed horizontal and vertical lines illustrate the rise and run used to calculate the slope. A table beside the graph lists measured values of mass in kilograms, weight in newtons, and displacement in meters. As mass increases from 0.000 to 0.500 kilograms, weight increases from 0.00 to 4.90 newtons and displacement increases from 0.000 to 0.127 meters. Below, springs hang vertically from a fixed support. One spring has no mass attached and is labeled x equals zero. Another spring holds a block labeled m1 with downward weight arrow w1 and displacement labeled x1. A third spring holds a larger block labeled m2 with downward weight arrow w2 and greater displacement labeled x2, showing that larger weight produces a larger stretch in the spring.

Two horizontal surfaces are shown in contact to illustrate how normal force affects microscopic contact between rough surfaces. In the upper example, labeled small normal force, a block rests lightly on another surface. The surfaces appear uneven at a microscopic scale, with small bumps and valleys. A downward arrow labeled N represents the normal force exerted by the top block on the lower surface, while an upward arrow labeled N represents the equal normal force from the lower surface pushing up on the block. Because the normal force is small, the surfaces touch only at a few of the highest bumps, leaving gaps between most of the irregularities. In the lower example, labeled large normal force, the top block presses down more strongly on the lower surface. The downward arrow labeled N is larger, indicating a greater normal force, and the upward arrow labeled N shows the larger supporting force from the lower surface. The stronger compression pushes the two rough surfaces closer together, causing more of the microscopic bumps to press against each other. This increases the real area of contact between the surfaces, illustrating how a larger normal force causes greater interlocking of surface irregularities.

A microscopic model of friction showing atomic layers of two surfaces interacting as one surface slides across another. The lower surface is represented by a tightly packed lattice of atoms arranged in repeating rows. Above it is a slanted probe composed of several layers of atoms stacked in rows. An arrow at the top labeled motion of probe indicates the probe sliding horizontally across the surface. At the contact region between the two materials, atoms from the probe and the surface interact. A label reading atomic and molecular interactions points to this region where the atoms from both surfaces come close together. These interactions create resistance to motion. A horizontal arrow labeled friction, f, points opposite the direction of sliding along the interface between the probe and the surface, indicating that friction acts to oppose the motion of the probe.

Two circular masses connected by a straight rod illustrate forces acting between two objects. On the left is a smaller circle labeled m with its center of mass marked CM. On the right is a larger circle labeled M with its center of mass also marked CM. A straight rod connects the two centers, and the distance between them is labeled r. Arrows labeled F are drawn along the rod pointing in opposite directions. The arrow near the smaller mass points toward the larger mass, indicating the force acting on the smaller object. The arrow near the larger mass points toward the smaller mass, indicating the force acting on the larger object. A dashed line along the rod shows the line connecting the two centers of mass.

A bright star on the left is shown being pulled toward a black hole on the right. The star is drawn as a large glowing orange sphere. Streams of material extend from the star toward the black hole, forming a long curved trail. The material spirals around the black hole in a circular disk before moving inward. The black hole is labeled “Black hole.” The background shows a purple space scene with many small white stars.

Three panels show the positions of Earth, the Moon, and the Sun during different tidal conditions. In the top panel labeled (a), Earth is shown with an elongated blue water bulge around it labeled spring tide. The Moon is positioned above Earth, and the Sun is shown to the right. The water bulge around Earth is stretched along a vertical direction. In the middle panel labeled (b), Earth again shows an elongated water bulge labeled spring tide. The Moon is positioned below Earth while the Sun remains to the right. In the bottom panel labeled (c), Earth shows a more circular water shape labeled neap tide. The Moon is positioned to the left of Earth, forming a right angle relative to the Sun, which remains on the right side of the diagram.

Earth is shown in the center surrounded by a blue ring representing ocean water. The water bulges outward on the left and right sides of Earth, and both of these regions are labeled high tide. The regions at the top and bottom of Earth are labeled low tide. A curved arrow inside Earth indicates the rotation of the planet. To the right of Earth is the Moon, shown as a gray circular body with crater markings. A green arrow points upward next to the Moon. The figure also includes the note “not to scale.”

Two panels show an object moving along a curved circular path. In each panel, the path is drawn as part of a circle with a point on the curve representing the object. A green arrow labeled v shows the velocity of the object tangent to the path. A red arrow labeled r points from the center of the circle toward the object, representing the radius. A red arrow labeled f points toward the center of the circular path, indicating the inward force acting on the object. The top panel labels the force as large f with a larger radius r. Text between the panels states that f equals Fc and is parallel to ac since Fc equals m ac. The lower panel shows the object moving with the same velocity v but with a smaller radius r. The inward force arrow is labeled large Fc, and the text notes small r and same v.

A car is shown driving on a banked road surface tilted at an angle labeled theta. The car is angled relative to the horizontal road, and arrows represent the forces acting on it. A downward arrow labeled w represents the weight of the car acting vertically downward. A diagonal arrow labeled N represents the normal force exerted by the road on the car. The normal force is resolved into two components. The vertical component is labeled N cos theta equals w. The horizontal component is labeled N sin theta equals Fc equals Fnet, pointing toward the center of the curve. The diagram shows the angle theta between the banked surface and the horizontal road.

Three panels compare forces acting on two different objects. In the left panel labeled (a), a person pushes a rectangular box with both hands. The box is labeled mass m1. An arrow labeled F points forward from the box, and an arrow labeled a1 points in the same direction indicating acceleration. In the right panel labeled (b), the same person pushes the back of a car labeled mass m2. An arrow labeled F points forward from the car, and an arrow labeled a2 indicates acceleration in the same direction. Below these panels, text states that the free body diagrams for both objects are the same. Two simple free body diagrams are shown, each consisting of a single horizontal arrow labeled F pointing to the right from a dot representing the object.

A roller coaster track forms a vertical loop supported by a wooden framework. The track begins on a horizontal section, curves upward, and forms a complete circular loop before returning to the horizontal track. A small roller coaster car with several riders is shown moving upward along the right side of the loop. An arrow next to the car points upward along the track indicating the direction of motion. The track is colored green and the support structure underneath is a grid-like wooden frame.

Two cars travel around a curved road. The road bends left around a grassy area. A blue car follows an inner curved route labeled Path 1, while a red car follows a wider curved route labeled Path 2. Black arrows show the direction each car travels along the curve. The dashed white line in the center marks the lane division of the road.

A small object attached to a string moves in a circular path on the surface of a rectangular table. The string extends from the object toward a point near the center of the circle, and the radius of the circle is labeled r. A black circular arrow shows the direction of motion around the path. The word “String” labels the line connecting the object to the center point. The table is shown with four legs supporting the flat surface.

A rotating merry-go-round is shown from above as a circular platform. A curved arrow labeled omega indicates the direction of rotation around the platform. A person stands near the edge of the platform holding a vertical rope. A point labeled P marks a position on the rotating surface near the person. From point P, three arrows labeled A, B, and C show possible directions of motion away from that point. The label below the figure reads “Merry-go-round’s rotating frame of reference.”

A person performs a squat while lifting a barbell across their shoulders. Two positions of the lifter are shown: one lower squat position and one higher standing position, indicating upward motion during the lift. Black arrows next to the lifter point upward to show the direction of movement. To the right of the lifter, two vertical arrows are shown. One orange arrow labeled F points upward representing the applied force. A black arrow labeled d also points upward representing displacement. Below the arrows, the equation “W = F d” is written, showing the relationship between work, force, and displacement.

Three examples illustrate the relationship between force, displacement, and work. (a) A person pushes a lawn mower forward across grass. A horizontal arrow labeled force points in the direction of motion, and another arrow labeled displacement shows the distance moved in the same direction. Above the diagram, the relationship work equals force multiplied by displacement is shown. (b) A person stands holding a briefcase at their side. An upward arrow labeled force shows the force applied to hold the briefcase, but the displacement is labeled displacement equals zero, indicating no movement. (c) A person walks forward while carrying a briefcase. A horizontal arrow labeled displacement shows the direction of motion, while a vertical arrow labeled force shows the upward force supporting the briefcase. The angle between the force and displacement is labeled theta equals ninety degrees.

A box of mass thirty kilograms sits on a horizontal surface with small rollers. Several forces acting on the box are shown with arrows. A rightward arrow labeled applied force equals one hundred twenty newtons pushes the box to the right. A leftward arrow labeled friction force equals five newtons opposes the motion. A horizontal arrow to the right labeled displacement equals zero point eight zero zero meters shows the distance the box moves. Two vertical forces are also shown: a downward arrow labeled weight and an upward arrow labeled normal force from the surface supporting the box.

A vertical cross-section of a multi-story building shows two ways for a person to reach a higher floor. On the right side, a person walks up a staircase that zigzags between floors. A vertical bracket labeled h marks the total vertical height gained from the bottom floor to the upper floor. On the left side, an elevator system is shown with a cable running over a pulley at the top of the building. A counterweight and elevator car move vertically between the bottom point labeled A and an upper point labeled B. The diagram compares different paths for reaching the same height in the building.

Two diagrams show a cuckoo clock with hanging weights connected by chains. (a) In the first diagram, a person pulls down on the chain to raise a weight. A downward arrow labeled force shows the applied force pulling the chain downward through a distance labeled displacement. The weight of mass m rises upward through a height labeled h. The diagram shows the relationships input energy equals work and work equals mass times gravitational acceleration times height. (b) In the second diagram, the raised weight slowly descends as the clock operates. The energy released by the falling weight is labeled output energy equals mass times gravitational acceleration times height.

A roller coaster track is shown with a cart starting at the top of a hill labeled Start. The cart begins from rest with initial velocity equals zero. The track descends into a valley and then rises again to a smaller hill before reaching a flat section labeled Finish. A vertical arrow from the starting point down to the lowest point indicates a height difference of twenty-five meters. Another vertical arrow shows a height difference of twenty meters between the lowest point and the finishing height. The final velocity at the finish is labeled velocity equals unknown. The diagram illustrates motion along the track and changes in height used to analyze energy conservation.

A graph shows the relationship between applied force and displacement for a spring. The vertical axis is labeled applied force equals applied force, and the horizontal axis is labeled displacement equals x. A straight line starts at the origin and rises linearly to the right, representing the relationship force equals spring constant multiplied by displacement. The triangular shaded area under the line between zero and displacement x represents the work done stretching the spring. A dashed horizontal line marks the midpoint force labeled applied force equals one-half multiplied by spring constant multiplied by displacement. On the right side, two methods are shown for calculating the work: Method A states work equals one-half multiplied by base multiplied by height, which equals one-half multiplied by spring constant multiplied by displacement multiplied by displacement, giving work equals one-half multiplied by spring constant multiplied by displacement squared. Method B states work equals force multiplied by displacement, where force equals one-half multiplied by spring constant multiplied by displacement, resulting in work equals one-half multiplied by spring constant multiplied by displacement squared.

Three panels show how a compressed spring launches a small object. (a) A device containing a spring is attached to the back of a gun-like launcher. The spring is uncompressed and the projectile sits in front of it. (b) The spring is compressed by pushing the projectile backward. Text indicates that work is done to compress the spring. The compression distance is labeled x, and the projectile of mass m is shown next to the compressed spring. The stored energy is labeled elastic potential energy. (c) The spring expands and pushes the projectile forward. An arrow shows the projectile moving to the right with velocity v, and the energy of the moving projectile is labeled kinetic energy.

A person moves up a staircase, illustrating changes in energy and forces. At the bottom of the stairs, the person stands with initial velocity equals zero. A downward arrow labeled weight equals mass multiplied by gravitational acceleration shows the gravitational force acting downward. The normal force from the ground is labeled normal force equals negative weight. A staircase rises to a higher platform. A vertical arrow labeled h indicates the height gained while climbing the stairs. At the top of the stairs, the person is shown running forward with velocity labeled v sub i. The energy at this point is labeled kinetic energy plus gravitational potential energy, indicating the combination of motion and height-related energy.

A car travels along a hilly road illustrating changes in motion. On the left, the car moves downhill as indicated by the label coasts down hill. It then moves upward over a small crest labeled coasts up over crest, followed by another descent labeled coasts down hill. On the far right, the car reaches a flat area and stops at a gas pump, labeled stops for gasoline. Arrows above each section indicate the direction of motion along the changing slope of the road.

A graph shows force as a function of time. The vertical axis is labeled force, and the horizontal axis is labeled time. A curved line labeled actual force rises from zero at time t one, reaches a peak, and then decreases back to zero at time t two. A shaded region under the curve represents the total effect of the varying force over time. A dashed horizontal line labeled effective force indicates a constant force that would produce the same overall effect. A rectangular shaded area between t one and t two represents this constant effective force, equal in area to the region under the curved line.

Two panels compare the motion of two cars before and after an interaction. In the top panel labeled before, two cars are shown moving to the right. The first car has mass m one and velocity v one, and the second car has mass m two and velocity v two. The system of interest includes both cars, and the net force on the system is labeled net force equals zero. The total momentum is shown as momentum one plus momentum two equals total momentum. In the bottom panel labeled after, the same two cars continue moving to the right with new velocities labeled v one prime and v two prime. The total momentum is shown as momentum one prime plus momentum two prime equals total momentum, indicating that momentum is conserved for the system.

A rocket moves along a curved trajectory, shown in two stages labeled before and after. In each stage, the rocket is enclosed in an oval labeled system of interest, with its center of mass marked as center of mass. The rocket follows a curved path through space. In the horizontal direction, the net force is labeled net force in the x direction equals zero, indicating no horizontal force. In the vertical direction, the net force is labeled net force in the y direction equals mass times gravitational acceleration, not equal to zero, showing that gravity acts downward. Momentum components are shown with arrows. The horizontal momentum is labeled momentum in the x direction is constant, while the vertical momentum is labeled momentum in the y direction is not constant. On the right, this is summarized as momentum in the x direction after equals momentum in the x direction before, and momentum in the y direction after is not equal to momentum in the y direction before.

Two panels show a collision between two blocks on a frictionless surface, illustrating conservation of momentum and kinetic energy. In the top panel labeled before, a smaller block of mass m one moves to the right with velocity v one, and a larger block of mass m two moves to the right with velocity v two. The system of interest includes both blocks, and the net force is labeled net force equals zero. The total momentum is shown as momentum one plus momentum two equals total momentum. In the bottom panel labeled after, the blocks move with new velocities labeled v one prime and v two prime. The total momentum is shown as momentum one prime plus momentum two prime equals total momentum. A statement indicates an elastic collision, where kinetic energy one prime plus kinetic energy two prime equals kinetic energy one plus kinetic energy two, meaning total kinetic energy is conserved.

Two panels show a collision on a frictionless ice surface involving a hockey puck and a goalie. In the left panel labeled before, a puck of mass m one moves to the right with velocity v one, while the goalie of mass m two is initially at rest with velocity equals zero. The system of interest includes both objects, and the net force is labeled net force equals zero. The total momentum is shown as momentum one equals total momentum. In the right panel labeled after, the puck and goalie move together to the right with a shared velocity labeled v prime, indicating they stick together after the collision. The total momentum is shown as momentum one prime plus momentum two prime equals total momentum, while the internal kinetic energy is labeled kinetic energy internal after is less than kinetic energy internal before, indicating an inelastic collision where kinetic energy is not conserved.

Two panels show a head-on collision between two identical blocks on a frictionless surface. In the left panel labeled before, two blocks each of mass m move toward each other with equal speeds but opposite directions. One block has velocity v to the right, and the other has velocity negative v to the left. The total momentum is labeled momentum one plus momentum two equals zero, and the internal kinetic energy is labeled kinetic energy internal equals mass times velocity squared. The system of interest includes both blocks, and the net force is labeled net force equals zero. In the right panel labeled after, the two blocks stick together and remain at rest with velocity equals zero. The total momentum is labeled momentum one prime plus momentum two prime equals zero, and the internal kinetic energy is labeled kinetic energy internal equals zero, showing that kinetic energy is lost while momentum is conserved.

Two panels illustrate an interaction between two blocks connected by a spring on a frictionless surface. In the top panel labeled before, block one with mass m one moves to the right with velocity v one, and block two with mass m two moves to the left with velocity v two. A compressed spring is positioned between them. The system of interest includes both blocks and the spring, and the net force is labeled net force equals zero. The total momentum is shown as momentum one plus momentum two equals total momentum. In the bottom panel labeled after, the spring has expanded (uncoiled), pushing the blocks apart. Block one moves to the left with velocity v one prime, and block two moves to the right with velocity v two prime. The total momentum is labeled momentum one prime plus momentum two prime equals total momentum, showing momentum is conserved. The internal kinetic energy is labeled kinetic energy internal prime is greater than kinetic energy internal, indicating that energy stored in the spring has been converted into kinetic energy.

Five subfigures labeled (a) through (e) show a block attached to a horizontal spring on a frictionless surface, along with corresponding free body diagrams. In (a), the block of mass m is stretched to the right at position x equals positive X with velocity equal to zero. A force is applied to the left, representing the spring force pulling the block back toward equilibrium. The free body diagram shows three forces: normal force upward, weight downward, and spring force to the left. In (b), the block passes through the equilibrium position x equals zero moving to the left with maximum speed, labeled negative v maximum. The net force is zero at this point, and the free body diagram shows only the normal force upward and weight downward. In (c), the block is compressed to the left at position x equals negative X with velocity equal to zero. The spring force acts to the right, pushing the block back toward equilibrium. The free body diagram shows normal force upward, weight downward, and spring force to the right. In (d), the block again passes through equilibrium x equals zero, now moving to the right with maximum speed, labeled positive v maximum. The net force is zero, and the free body diagram shows only normal force upward and weight downward. In (e), the block is stretched again to the right at position x equals positive X with velocity equal to zero. The spring force acts to the left, repeating the motion cycle.

A multi-part illustration shows the motion of a mass attached to a vertical spring undergoing simple harmonic motion. At the top, several snapshots show the mass moving up and down between three positions: x equals positive X (maximum upward displacement), x equals zero (equilibrium), and x equals negative X (maximum downward displacement). Arrows indicate the direction of motion at different points. Below, three graphs show how motion changes over time. The first is position versus time, a sinusoidal curve oscillating between positive X and negative X, with amplitude labeled amplitude X and period labeled T. The second is velocity versus time, with values labeled positive v maximum and negative v maximum. The third is acceleration versus time, with values labeled positive a maximum and negative a maximum. At the bottom, the spring is shown at its unstrained length, at equilibrium, and at positions above and below equilibrium corresponding to x equals positive X, x equals zero, and x equals negative X.

A mass labeled m hangs from a vertical spring and oscillates up and down between positions marked x equals positive X, x equals zero, and x equals negative X. The vertical motion of the mass is shown by an arrow indicating displacement. Attached to the mass is a pen that records its motion on a moving strip of paper. The paper moves horizontally to the right, labeled motion of paper, while the pen traces a sinusoidal curve. The horizontal axis is labeled time t, and one full cycle is marked as the period T. The resulting wave represents position as a function of time for the oscillating mass.

Three side-by-side illustrations show a mass suspended from a string being driven up and down by a hand at different frequencies. In the left case labeled low f, the hand moves the string slowly up and down, and the mass oscillates with a small amplitude labeled two times X. The motion is limited, and the mass does not reach large displacements. In the middle case labeled f naught, the driving frequency matches the system’s natural frequency. The mass oscillates with the largest amplitude, shown as a much greater vertical distance labeled two times X, indicating resonance. In the right case labeled high f, the hand moves the string rapidly, and the mass again oscillates with a small amplitude labeled two times X, showing reduced motion at frequencies higher than the natural frequency.

A black-and-white photograph shows a long suspension bridge with visible vertical cables and tall support towers. The bridge deck appears significantly distorted, bending and twisting instead of remaining straight. The roadway curves downward and then upward in a wave-like shape, indicating large oscillations. Trees and landscape are visible below the bridge. The image illustrates structural instability caused by oscillations, where resonance leads to large amplitude motion in the bridge.

A graph shows amplitude X on the vertical axis and driving frequency f on the horizontal axis. Three curves represent different levels of damping: small damping, medium damping, and heavy damping. All curves peak near the natural frequency labeled f naught. The curve for small damping has the highest and narrowest peak, indicating a large amplitude at resonance. The medium damping curve has a lower and wider peak. The heavy damping curve has the lowest and widest peak, showing reduced amplitude and less sharp resonance. The horizontal axis includes markers at f naught over two, f naught, and three f naught over two. The graph shows that increasing damping decreases the maximum amplitude and broadens the range of frequencies over which oscillations occur.

A sinusoidal water wave is shown moving to the right with velocity v sub w. The horizontal distance between two repeating points on the wave is labeled wavelength lambda. The vertical distance from the equilibrium (dashed line) to a crest is labeled X, and to a trough is labeled negative X. The total vertical distance from crest to trough is labeled two times X. A duck floating on the surface moves up and down with the wave but does not travel horizontally with it, while a fish below the surface remains relatively unaffected by the wave motion.

A guitar is connected to an amplifier and speaker, producing sound waves that travel through the air toward a person. The speaker emits curved wavefronts spreading outward. A close-up shows the guitar string vibrating up and down, indicated by vertical motion arrows. Another close-up shows air particles vibrating back and forth in the same direction as the wave travels, showing longitudinal motion. The person places a hand near the speaker to feel the vibrations, showing that sound waves involve oscillations of air particles rather than movement of the particles over long distances.

Two sinusoidal waves labeled wave one and wave two are shown one above the other. Each wave has amplitude labeled X and negative X. At corresponding positions, one wave has a positive displacement X while the other has a negative displacement negative X. Below them, a third line labeled resultant is a straight horizontal line at zero. Vertical lines mark specific positions along the waves where the values are labeled zero.

Two sinusoidal waves labeled wave one and wave two are shown one above the other. Both waves have amplitude labeled X and negative X, and their peaks and troughs align at the same horizontal positions. Below them, a third wave labeled resultant is shown. This wave has larger peaks and troughs with amplitude labeled two times X and negative two times X. Vertical lines mark specific positions along the waves where corresponding points line up.

A sequence of five panels shows two sinusoidal waves labeled wave one and wave two, along with a third line labeled resultant, at different times. The panels are labeled t equals zero, t equals one quarter T, t equals one half T, t equals three quarters T, and t equals T. Arrows above the waves indicate motion along the horizontal direction. In each panel, the two waves shift relative to each other. The resultant below changes shape over time, alternating between large amplitude peaks and troughs, a flat line at zero, and intermediate waveforms depending on the alignment of the two waves.

Three horizontal wave plots are shown. The top plot labeled wave one is a smooth sinusoidal wave with a large wavelength and amplitude. The middle plot labeled wave two is a sinusoidal wave with a much shorter wavelength and smaller amplitude. The bottom plot labeled resultant shows a combined waveform with varying amplitude. The shape includes regions of larger and smaller oscillations, reflecting the combination of the two waves.

Three horizontal wave plots are shown over time. The top plot labeled wave one is a sinusoidal wave with frequency f one and wavelength lambda one. The middle plot labeled wave two is a sinusoidal wave with a slightly different frequency f two and wavelength lambda two. The bottom plot labeled resultant shows a wave whose amplitude varies over time. Sections are marked destructive where the amplitude is small and constructive where the amplitude is large. The horizontal axis is labeled time.

Two side-by-side illustrations show standing wave patterns on a string fixed at both ends with length L. On the left, labeled first overtone, the string forms two loops between the fixed ends. Points labeled nodes appear where there is no motion. The wavelength is labeled lambda two equals L, and the frequency is shown as f two equals wave speed over L equals two times f one. On the right, labeled second overtone, the string forms three loops with additional nodes between the ends. The wavelength is labeled lambda three equals two over three times L, and the frequency is shown as f three equals three times wave speed over two times L equals three times f one.

A string fixed at both ends with length L forms a standing wave in the fundamental mode. The string has one loop with maximum displacement at the center labeled antinode and zero displacement at both ends labeled nodes. The wavelength is labeled lambda one equals two times L, and the frequency is shown as f one equals wave speed over two times L. A dashed curve to the right shows the motion of the string over time as it oscillates.

Sound waves are shown traveling through air toward a human ear as alternating regions labeled compression and rarefaction. The waves are drawn as concentric patterns moving inward. Inside the ear canal, the eardrum is labeled with area A. A force labeled F equals pressure times area acts on the eardrum, with arrows indicating motion back and forth as the pressure changes.

A longitudinal sound wave is shown with alternating regions of higher and lower pressure on either side of a central object. Curved lines indicate compressions and rarefactions moving through the medium. At the center, a small element is shown oscillating back and forth horizontally with arrows indicating motion. Below, a sinusoidal graph represents pressure variation with position. The vertical axis is labeled gauge pressure (pressure relative to atmospheric pressure) and includes atmospheric pressure (the baseline pressure of the surrounding air) as a reference level. The horizontal axis is labeled position (distance along the direction of wave travel), showing the pressure oscillating above and below equilibrium.

A tuning fork is shown at the center emitting circular sound waves outward on both sides. The waves are drawn as expanding arcs with alternating shaded regions representing compressions and rarefactions. Arrows on each side indicate wave propagation away from the tuning fork, labeled wave speed (v). A horizontal distance between two similar points on the wave is marked and labeled wavelength (lambda).

A speaker box is shown emitting sound waves from two openings. From the top opening, closely spaced circular wavefronts are drawn and labeled high frequency (f), small wavelength (lambda). From the bottom opening, widely spaced circular wavefronts are drawn and labeled small frequency (f), large wavelength (lambda).

A red car is shown moving to the right between two people labeled X on the left and Y on the right. Circular wavefronts are drawn around the car, with the wavefronts compressed in front of the car and spread out behind it. Numbered wavefronts from 1 to 5 are shown, with closer spacing toward the front of the car and wider spacing toward the back. Diagonal lines labeled 1 to 5 extend from the car toward the left, indicating the positions of earlier emitted waves.

A red car is shown centered between two people labeled X on the left and Y on the right. Circular wavefronts are evenly spaced around the car, forming concentric circles that expand outward symmetrically in all directions.

A red car is centered between two people labeled X on the left and Y on the right. Concentric circular wavefronts expand outward evenly from the car. Both people are shown in motion, with repeated outlines indicating movement away from the car on both sides.

An airplane is shown moving to the right at the front of a V-shaped pattern formed by shaded regions extending behind it. The shaded regions form two angled boundaries that meet at the airplane, creating a cone-like shape. Three people are standing on the ground below the shaded area, with one person positioned inside the cone and another outside, marked with a question mark.

A rectangular speaker box is shown with two circular openings on its front. From the top opening, several closely spaced circular wavefronts extend outward. From the bottom opening, fewer, more widely spaced circular wavefronts extend outward. Text near the top wavefronts reads high frequency, small wavelength, and text near the bottom wavefronts reads small frequency, large wavelength.

A curved circular path is shown with an object moving along it. A vector labeled velocity (v) is drawn tangent to the path. Two acceleration vectors are shown at the same point: one labeled tangential acceleration (a sub t, where t is a subscript indicating tangential) pointing along the direction of motion, and another labeled centripetal acceleration (a sub c, where c is a subscript indicating centripetal) pointing inward toward the center of the circle. A line labeled radius (r) extends from the center of the circle to the object. Text below states that tangential acceleration affects magnitude of velocity and centripetal acceleration affects direction of velocity.

A circular path is shown with an object moving along the edge. A vector labeled velocity (v) is drawn tangent to the path in the direction of motion. A vector labeled tangential acceleration (a sub t, where t is a subscript indicating tangential) is shown pointing along the direction of motion, and another vector labeled centripetal acceleration (a sub c, where c is a subscript indicating centripetal) is shown pointing inward toward the center of the circle. A line labeled radius (r) extends from the center of the circle to the object.

A bicycle wheel is shown with a hand at the top pulling on the tire. An arrow labeled force (F) points tangentially along the rim where the hand is applying the pull. At the center of the wheel, an arrow labeled angular acceleration (alpha) indicates rotation about the axle. The spokes and circular rim of the bike tire are visible, along with part of the bicycle frame connected to the wheel.

A collection of diagrams shows different objects with axes of rotation and their corresponding moments of inertia. For a hoop about its central axis, the moment of inertia is I equals mass times radius squared. For an annular cylinder or ring, the moment of inertia is I equals one half times mass times the sum of inner radius squared and outer radius squared. For a solid cylinder or disk about its central axis, the moment of inertia is I equals one half times mass times radius squared. For a solid cylinder or disk about a central diameter, the moment of inertia is I equals one quarter times mass times radius squared plus one twelfth times mass times length squared. For a thin rod about an axis through its center perpendicular to its length, the moment of inertia is I equals one twelfth times mass times length squared. For a thin rod about an axis through one end perpendicular to its length, the moment of inertia is I equals one third times mass times length squared. For a solid sphere about any diameter, the moment of inertia is I equals two fifths times mass times radius squared. For a thin spherical shell about any diameter, the moment of inertia is I equals two thirds times mass times radius squared. For a hoop about any diameter, the moment of inertia is I equals one half times mass times radius squared. For a rectangular slab about an axis through its center perpendicular to its surface, the moment of inertia is I equals mass times the sum of a squared and b squared divided by twelve.

Three cylindrical objects labeled one, two, and three are shown at the top of an inclined plane at height h. Arrows indicate each object moving down the incline, with dashed outlines showing their positions further along the slope. The cylinders are oriented horizontally and move in parallel paths down the surface, with their positions shifting downward along the incline.

Two related images are shown. In the top image labeled (a), a helicopter rotor is viewed from above with multiple blades extending outward from a central hub. The blades are shown in several faded positions indicating rotation, with curved arrows showing the direction of motion. One blade is highlighted, and a distance from the center to the tip is labeled 4.0 meters. In the bottom image labeled (b), a helicopter is flying above water with a person suspended below it by a cable, hanging beneath the aircraft.

Two side-by-side images show a figure skater spinning on one foot.
